narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 44.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to raise concerns about your long-term success. As a male student from a white background planning to attend part-time with a high family income, above-average LSAT score of 38, undergraduate GPA of 2.8, and bottom-decile rankings in both your first-year and third-year school grades, you have a mixed academic profile compared to typical applicants. Most students rank around the middle of their class in those early and later years, with undergraduate GPAs averaging 3.21, so your lower performance there stood out significantly. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your first-year class decile, which pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure more than any other factor. Coming in second was your third-year decile, also in the bottom 10% of your school, reinforcing that concern about your grades. Your high family income level was the next biggest influence, surprisingly nudging the outlook toward failure in the model's eyes, perhaps because it doesn't directly offset academic struggles. Even your solid LSAT score, better than the average of 36.7, contributed slightly toward the failure prediction rather than helping as much as it might for others. Your racial background as white, which is common in the applicant pool, had a moderate push in the same direction, followed by your undergraduate GPA, part-time enrollment plans (rarer than full-time), and gender as male, which had the least impact overall. In essence, your low school rankings overshadowed your strengths, unlike typical students where mid-range grades balance out other traits for a stronger pass prediction. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger high school or undergrad performance if reapplying soon, or consider full-time study to align with the majority who succeed that way. Retaking the LSAT to push even higher could help too, as it showed some positive signal despite the prediction. With targeted improvements in grades and study habits, you could shift the model's view toward expecting a bar pass. Hang in there—many students overcome early hurdles like this with persistence.
